article_title: Australian police officers can be tracked due to a security flaw in tasers and body-worn cameras
core_thesis: Axon tasers and body-worn cameras used by Australian police have a Bluetooth security flaw: they broadcast fixed MAC addresses, allowing anyone with a smartphone to track officers' real-time locations. Despite warnings from a hacker and internal assessments confirming the risk, police agencies have not remediated the issue, leaving officers vulnerable to ambush or surveillance.
detailed_summary: An anonymous hacker discovered that Axon's Bluetooth-enabled tasers and body-worn cameras emit static MAC addresses, unlike modern smartphones that randomize them. He built proof-of-concept software that could detect and locate officers from up to 400 meters away using publicly available apps. He alerted Victoria Police in 2024, warning that organized crime could scale tracking with cheap Bluetooth scanners. Victoria Police internally assessed the risk as real but dismissed it after consulting Axon, claiming no issues. Four Corners confirmed that police agencies in NSW, Queensland, WA, Tasmania, NT, and the ACT use vulnerable Axon devices (T7/T10 tasers and body cameras) and have not informed officers or taken action. Axon acknowledges the flaw in fine print but states it cannot be patched due to hardware limitations; a full redesign is required. The vulnerability is already exploited overseas, with US Border Patrol told to stop using Axon cameras. Criminologist Emma Ryan notes police are 'captured' by Axon's monopoly, and the flaw could be weaponized for ambushes or evading raids.

entities.name: Axon
entities.role: US tech giant manufacturing tasers and body-worn cameras; devices have Bluetooth flaw

entities.name: Victoria Police
entities.role: Australian police force that received hacker's warning but dismissed the risk

entities.name: Four Corners
entities.role: ABC investigative journalism program that broke the story

entities.name: Emma Ryan
entities.role: Criminologist researching tasers; commented on police reliance on Axon

entities.name: Anonymous hacker
entities.role: Discovered and demonstrated the Bluetooth tracking vulnerability

key_data_points: Axon tasers and body-worn cameras broadcast fixed Bluetooth MAC addresses, unlike modern phones that randomize them (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: this is a passive reconnaissance vector – attackers can identify and track police without active scanning).

The hacker's proof-of-concept software could detect officers from up to 400 meters away using a phone or laptop (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates how easily location data can be harvested; similar techniques could be used to track security personnel or assets).

Victoria Police internally assessed the risk as real but dismissed it after speaking with Axon, and did not inform frontline officers (🟢 relevant to your interest in security culture: shows organizational denial of vulnerabilities).

Axon's 'Stealth Mode' does not disable Bluetooth/Wi-Fi radio signals; the flaw is hardware-based and cannot be patched – a full redesign is required (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: indicates that devices are permanently vulnerable; physical access or replacement is needed to mitigate).
US Border Patrol was reportedly told to stop using Axon body cameras in the field due to similar security risks (🟢 relevant to your global perspective: the issue is not isolated to Australia).

The hacker warned that organized crime could deploy dozens of cheap Bluetooth scanners to create a real-time police tracking map (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: this is a scalable attack scenario; you could simulate such a system for red team exercises).
conclusions: The Bluetooth MAC address flaw in Axon devices is a serious operational security risk for police, enabling real-time tracking by anyone with a smartphone. The fact that police agencies have not remediated it suggests a systemic failure to prioritize officer safety over vendor relationships.

From a red team perspective, this vulnerability is a goldmine for reconnaissance: it allows passive, long-distance identification of law enforcement personnel, which could be used to plan ambushes, avoid raids, or map police presence. The hacker's proof-of-concept shows how easily it can be weaponized.

The hardware-level nature of the flaw means software patches cannot fix it; only device recall or replacement can mitigate the risk. This highlights the importance of considering security at the hardware design stage, especially for devices used in sensitive roles.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your Red Teaming interests as it details a real-world Bluetooth tracking vulnerability that can be exploited for passive reconnaissance against law enforcement. The hacker's methodology – using publicly available apps to detect fixed MAC addresses – is a technique you can replicate or adapt for red team exercises targeting physical security or personnel tracking. The article also underscores the importance of MAC address randomization as a privacy/security feature, which you can apply when assessing IoT or wearable devices. As a programmer, you could build a similar proof-of-concept scanner to test your own devices or demonstrate the risk to clients. The organizational failure to act despite warnings is a cautionary tale about security culture and vendor lock-in.
extra_advice: Consider building a small Bluetooth scanner using a Raspberry Pi and a Bluetooth dongle to passively log MAC addresses in your area. This would give you hands-on experience with the technique described and could be used in red team engagements to demonstrate physical tracking risks. Also, review Axon's security advisories for any disclosed mitigations – if you encounter Axon devices in a penetration test, this is a quick win for reconnaissance.
